deck.title: CCNP ENCOR 350-401 Memory Tables C
deck.description:
Exam version: ccnp-encor-350-401-v1.2
Memory Tables C

front.text: In EIGRP, what is the feasibility condition and why does it matter for selecting a feasible successor?

back.text: The feasibility condition states that a route can be considered a loop-free backup (feasible successor) only if the neighbor's reported distance (RD) is less than the local feasible distance (FD) to the destination. This guarantees the backup path does not loop back through the local router.

rows: First next-hop router on the lowest-metric (best) path
Lowest metric calculated locally to reach the destination
Metric advertised by the neighbor to reach the destination
Backup next hop where RD < FD (passes feasibility condition)

back.text: Exam tip: If RD >= FD, the route is NOT a feasible successor, even if it is a valid alternate path. EIGRP would have to go active and send queries to use it.

front.text: Name the five EIGRP packet types and the primary purpose of each.

rows: Search for an alternate path during convergence
Discover neighbors and detect loss of adjacency

front.text: An EIGRP route goes active and queries are sent to all neighbors. What does this indicate about the local topology table, and what is a common scaling concern?

back.text: Going active means EIGRP lost the successor and has no feasible successor in the topology table, so it must query neighbors to find an alternate loop-free path. The scaling concern is Stuck-In-Active (SIA): if neighbors fail to reply within the SIA timer, adjacencies can be reset. Summarization and stub routers limit query scope.
Bound the query domain with summary routes and EIGRP stub configuration to reduce SIA risk.

front.text: List the five OSPF packet types and what each is used for.

rows: Summarize LSDB contents during adjacency formation
Request specific LSAs believed to be stale

front.text: Compare the OSPF broadcast and point-to-point network types regarding DR/BDR and timers.

back.text: Network types must match between neighbors for adjacency to form on the same segment.

front.text: What are the key behaviors of the OSPF point-to-multipoint network type?

items: No DR/BDR is elected
Interface advertised as a /32 host route
Next-hop set to the outbound interface
Hello 30s / Dead 120s
Primarily used for hub-and-spoke topologies

front.text: Match these well-known multicast addresses to their use: 224.0.0.5, 224.0.0.6, 224.0.0.10, 224.0.0.13, 224.0.0.18, 224.0.0.102.
What multicast address range is reserved for Source Specific Multicast (SSM)?

back.text: 232.0.0.0 to 232.255.255.255 is the IANA-assigned SSM block. SSM uses (S,G) joins exclusively, so receivers specify both the source and the group, eliminating the need for an RP.

front.text: What is the function of an IGMPv2 General Membership Query (type 0x11) and where is it sent?

back.text: The router periodically sends a General Query to 224.0.0.1 (all-hosts) with the group address field set to 0.0.0.0 to determine if any receivers remain on the segment for any group. Receivers respond with membership reports for the groups they are interested in.

front.text: What is the RPF (Reverse Path Forwarding) interface and how is it selected if multiple paths tie?

back.text: The RPF interface is the interface with the lowest-cost unicast path (by AD then metric) toward the source (SPT) or RP (shared tree). If multiple interfaces have equal cost, the tiebreaker is the interface with the highest IP address of the next-hop neighbor.
Multicast packets failing the RPF check are dropped. RPF prevents multicast loops.

front.text: List the five PIM operating modes.

items: PIM Dense Mode (PIM-DM)
PIM Sparse Mode (PIM-SM)
PIM Sparse Dense Mode
PIM Source Specific Multicast (PIM-SSM)
PIM Bidirectional Mode (Bidir-PIM)

front.text: Which PIM control messages are unicast rather than sent to 224.0.0.13?

back.text: All other PIM messages (Hello, Join/Prune, Bootstrap, Assert, State Refresh, DF Election) go to 224.0.0.13.

front.text: Differentiate the FHR and LHR roles in a PIM-SM deployment.

rows: Encapsulates traffic in PIM Register messages to the RP
Sends PIM Join upstream toward RP or source

front.text: Compare the three QoS implementation models: Best Effort, IntServ, and DiffServ.

rows: Applications signal/reserve bandwidth (e.g., RSVP) per flow
Network classifies traffic into classes that get differentiated treatment

front.text: Name the four DiffServ Per-Hop Behaviors (PHBs) and a typical use for each.

rows: Backward compatibility with IP Precedence (first 3 bits)

front.text: In the Cisco token bucket model, what do CIR, Bc, and Tc represent, and how are they related?

items: CIR: Committed Information Rate in bps (the contracted average rate)
Bc: Committed Burst in bytes (max tokens; max traffic per Tc)
Tc: Time interval in ms during which Bc can be sent

back.text: Relationship: Tc (ms) = (Bc in bits / CIR in bps) * 1000, or equivalently Bc = CIR * (Tc/1000). One token = 1 byte (8 bits).

front.text: How does LLQ differ from CBWFQ, and when do you choose one over the other?
What four security services does IPsec provide?
List three key differences between IKEv1 and IKEv2.
Define EID, RLOC, ITR, and ETR in LISP.

rows: Endpoint Identifier â host's IP address inside a LISP site
Routing Locator â Internet/core-facing IP of an ETR
Ingress Tunnel Router â encapsulates EID traffic leaving a site
Egress Tunnel Router â de-encapsulates traffic entering a site

back.text: LISP separates identity (EID) from location (RLOC); MS/MR provide the mapping system.

items: Local LAN interfaces â bridge local hosts on the LAN segment
IP interface â core-facing; identifies the VTEP and is used for VXLAN encap/de-encap

items: VXLAN with Multicast underlay
VXLAN with static unicast tunnels

VXLAN with LISP control plane

rows: Direct user/endpoint connectivity into the network
Aggregates access; services and control boundary

items: Control plane node (LISP MS/MR EID-to-RLOC mapping)
Fabric border node (connects external Layer 3 networks)

Fabric WLC (connects APs and wireless endpoints)

rows: Forwards data plane traffic across WAN transports
Single pane of glass for management/monitoring

items: Standard stateful inspection
Integrated IPS

front.text: Compare inline (native) SGT tagging with SXP propagation.

rows: 16-bit SGT inserted into the L2 frame; preserved hop-by-hop
Requires ASIC/hardware support; non-supporting devices drop frame
TCP peer-to-peer protocol carries IP-to-SGT bindings

rows: Verifies who the user/device is before granting access
Defines what an authenticated user can do

Map HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) to CRUD operations and give a brief use case.

Replace an entire resource (e.g., update NTP server)
Append/modify part of a resource (e.g., add NTP server)

rows: DUAL using FD/RD with feasibility condition

rows: No (Type 7 instead, converted to Type 5 at ABR)

Describes routers attached to that segment
How to reach an ASBR in another area

front.text: Which IOS commands verify OSPF neighbors, the link-state database, and per-interface OSPF parameters?

back.text:
show ip ospf neighbor
show ip ospf database
show ip ospf interface brief
show ip ospf interface GigabitEthernet0/1
show ip ospf interface reveals network type, hello/dead, DR/BDR, cost, and area â great for adjacency troubleshooting.

front.text: Two routers are not forming an OSPF adjacency on a directly connected link. What parameters must match?

items: Same area ID
Same subnet/mask on the link
Matching hello and dead intervals
Same OSPF network type
Matching authentication (type and key)
Same MTU (DBD exchange will fail otherwise)
Matching stub area flags
Unique router IDs

front.text: List the first seven steps of the Cisco BGP best-path selection algorithm in order.

items: Prefer path with highest WEIGHT (Cisco proprietary, local to router)
Prefer path with highest LOCAL_PREF
Prefer locally originated route (network/redistribute/aggregate)
Prefer shortest AS_PATH
Prefer lowest ORIGIN code (IGP < EGP < Incomplete)
Prefer lowest MED (compared between paths from same neighbor AS)
Prefer eBGP over iBGP path

back.text: Mnemonic: We Love Oranges As Oranges Mean Pure Refreshment (Weight, LocalPref, Originated, AS-path, Origin, MED, eBGP/iBGP, IGP cost, Router-ID).

front.text: Show a minimal IOS configuration for an eBGP session between R1 (AS 65001) and R2 (AS 65002) on a directly connected link.

back.text:
! On R1
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 10.1.12.2 remote-as 65002
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  network 192.168.1.0 mask 255.255.255.0
 exit-address-family

! Verification
show ip bgp summary
show ip bgp neighbors 10.1.12.2
show ip bgp

front.text: What is Policy-Based Routing (PBR) and how does it differ from destination-based routing?

back.text: PBR uses a route-map applied to an interface to forward packets based on policy criteria such as source address, application, or packet size, instead of strictly the destination prefix in the routing table. It allows administrators to override normal routing for specific flows.

access-list 100 permit ip 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 any
!
route-map PBR-VOICE permit 10
 match ip address 100
 set ip next-hop 10.99.99.1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip policy route-map PBR-VOICE

front.text: Compare 802.1D STP port states with 802.1w RSTP port states.
Compare BPDU Guard, Root Guard, and Loop Guard.

rows: Any BPDU received on edge port
BPDUs stop arriving on non-designated port
Trunk uplinks to prevent unidirectional loops

front.text: Compare LACP, PAgP, and 'on' EtherChannel modes and indicate which combinations form a bundle.

back.text: passive-passive (LACP) and auto-auto (PAgP) will NOT form a channel â at least one side must initiate.

front.text: A trunk between two switches will not come up. Which mismatched parameters commonly prevent the trunk from forming?

items: Native VLAN mismatch (CDP/STP errors logged)
Encapsulation mismatch (e.g., dot1q vs ISL)
DTP mode mismatch (both ports set to dynamic auto)
Allowed VLAN list misalignment
VTP domain mismatch causing pruning issues

back.text:
show interfaces trunk
show interfaces switchport
show interfaces GigabitEthernet0/1 switchport

front.text: Compare HSRP, VRRP, and GLBP across vendor support, multicast address, and load balancing.
Compare static NAT, dynamic NAT, and PAT (NAT overload).

rows: Many inside hosts to a pool of outside addresses (1:1 at a time)
Limited public addresses, no port reuse
Many inside hosts share one or few outside addresses using unique L4 ports

front.text: How does PTP (IEEE 1588) differ from NTP, and when would you choose PTP?
What are the four main configuration components of Flexible NetFlow?

items: Flow record â defines key (match) and non-key (collect) fields
Flow exporter â defines collector destination, transport, version
Flow monitor â binds the record and exporter and is applied to interfaces
Flow sampler (optional) â samples packets to reduce CPU/bandwidth

back.text:
flow record FNF-REC
 match ipv4 source address
 match ipv4 destination address
 collect counter bytes
!
flow exporter FNF-EXP
 destination 10.10.10.1
 transport udp 2055
!
flow monitor FNF-MON
 record FNF-REC
 exporter FNF-EXP
!
interface Gi0/1
 ip flow monitor FNF-MON input

front.text: Compare SPAN, RSPAN, and ERSPAN.
Show a basic IP SLA ICMP echo configuration with object tracking that can be used by HSRP or static routes.

back.text:
ip sla 10
 icmp-echo 8.8.8.8 source-interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 frequency 5
!
ip sla schedule 10 life forever start-time now
!
track 1 ip sla 10 reachability
 delay down 10 up 5
Use 'show ip sla statistics' and 'show track' to verify operation and current state.

front.text: List the eight syslog severity levels from 0 to 7.
Compare SNMPv1, SNMPv2c, and SNMPv3.
Compare NETCONF and RESTCONF across transport, encoding, and operations.

rows: get, get-config, edit-config, copy-config, lock, etc.

front.text: What is YANG and what is its primary role in network programmability?

back.text: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation, RFC 7950) is a data modeling language used to define the structure of configuration and operational state data exchanged via NETCONF, RESTCONF, and gNMI. It provides a structured, validated, vendor-neutral schema that automation tools can use to read or modify device state.
YANG models can be open (OpenConfig, IETF) or native (Cisco-IOS-XE-native). Both can be served over NETCONF/RESTCONF.

front.text: What are the basic structural rules of valid JSON?

items: Data is in name/value pairs separated by colons
Keys must be strings in double quotes
Objects use {} ; arrays use []
Values can be string, number, boolean, null, object, or array
Items in objects/arrays are separated by commas; no trailing comma

front.text: What is the difference between a Python list, tuple, and dictionary?

back.text:
devices = [{'name': 'R1', 'os': 'IOS-XE'}, {'name': 'R2', 'os': 'NX-OS'}]
print(devices[0]['name'])  # R1

front.text: Write an EEM applet that logs a syslog message and saves the running config when an interface goes down.

back.text:
event manager applet IF-DOWN
 event syslog pattern "LINK-3-UPDOWN.*GigabitEthernet0/1.*down"
 action 1.0 syslog msg "Gi0/1 went down - saving config"
 action 2.0 cli command "enable"
 action 3.0 cli command "write memory"
Common EEM event detectors: syslog, snmp, timer, cli, none (manual run via 'event manager run').

front.text: Compare agent-based and agentless orchestration tools, and classify Ansible, Puppet, and Chef.

back.text: Agentless = nothing installed on managed device â often preferred for network gear.

front.text: What mechanisms commonly secure REST API access on Cisco platforms like Catalyst Center?

items: HTTPS (TLS) for transport encryption
Authentication via username/password or API tokens (often time-limited)
Authorization via role-based access control (RBAC)
Rate limiting / quotas to mitigate abuse
Input validation and audit logging on the API server

back.text: Catalyst Center uses a token obtained from /dna/system/api/v1/auth/token via Basic Auth, then passed in the X-Auth-Token header.

front.text: Differentiate numbered standard, numbered extended, named, PACL, and VACL.

rows: Standard, extended, or named MAC ACLs
Standard, extended, or named MAC ACLs

front.text: What is Control Plane Policing (CoPP) and why is it important?

back.text: CoPP applies a QoS policy (policer) to traffic destined for the device's control plane to protect the CPU from DoS-style attacks and excessive management traffic. Critical traffic such as routing protocols and SSH is permitted at higher rates while suspicious or unnecessary traffic is rate-limited or dropped.

class-map match-any CRITICAL
 match access-group name ROUTING
!
policy-map COPP
 class CRITICAL
  police 8000 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
!
control-plane
 service-policy input COPP

front.text: What are the three roles in an 802.1X deployment and how do they communicate?

rows: Cisco Secure Client, native OS supplicant
Supplicant via EAPoL; AAA server via RADIUS

front.text: What categories of REST APIs does Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center) expose?

items: Intent APIs â northbound APIs to express desired network state (provision, segmentation, policy)
Integration APIs â bidirectional APIs to integrate with third-party IT systems (ITSM, IPAM)
Multivendor SDK â southbound integration with non-Cisco devices
Events and Notifications â webhook-based async notifications

back.text: All Intent APIs require a token from /dna/system/api/v1/auth/token, sent in the X-Auth-Token header.

front.text: What is a VRF and how does it provide network virtualization on a single router?

back.text: A Virtual Routing and Forwarding (VRF) instance creates an independent routing/forwarding table on the same physical router, allowing overlapping IP address spaces and complete logical separation between tenants. Each interface is assigned to one VRF, and routing protocols can run per-VRF.

vrf definition CUSTOMER-A
 address-family ipv4
 exit-address-family
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 vrf forwarding CUSTOMER-A
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
!
show ip route vrf CUSTOMER-A

front.text: How does VRF-lite differ from MPLS L3VPN?

rows: Simple multi-tenant on a few devices

front.text: Show a basic GRE tunnel configuration between two routers.

back.text:
! R1
interface Tunnel0
 ip address 172.16.0.1 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/0
 tunnel destination 198.51.100.2
!
! R2
interface Tunnel0
 ip address 172.16.0.2 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/0
 tunnel destination 203.0.113.1
GRE provides multiprotocol encapsulation but no encryption. Combine with IPsec (tunnel protection ipsec profile X) for confidentiality.

front.text: What are the two methods to encrypt traffic over a GRE tunnel, and which is preferred?

rows: Legacy; ACL-defined interesting traffic, applied to physical interface
IPsec profile applied directly to the tunnel interface using 'tunnel protection ipsec profile'

front.text: Differentiate Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors with examples.

rows: On top of a host OS

front.text: What role does a virtual switch play in a hypervisor environment?

back.text: A virtual switch (vSwitch) runs inside the hypervisor and provides Layer 2 connectivity between VMs and between VMs and physical NICs (uplinks). It performs MAC learning, VLAN tagging, and policy enforcement, and may be controlled centrally (e.g., VMware vDS, Cisco Nexus 1000v) for consistent network policy across hosts.

front.text: What four components make up Cisco's Enterprise Network Functions Virtualization (ENFV) solution?

items: MANO (Management and Orchestration) â provided by Cisco DNA/Catalyst Center
VNFs (Virtual Network Functions) â virtual routers, firewalls, WLCs, etc.
NFVIS â Network Functions Virtualization Infrastructure Software (the hypervisor OS)
Hardware â x86 compute resources (e.g., ENCS platforms)

front.text: Compare local mode, FlexConnect, and autonomous AP deployments.

rows: Local switching at AP for some SSIDs; CAPWAP for others

front.text: What UDP ports does CAPWAP use, and what is each port for?
List the WLC discovery methods an AP uses, in priority order.

items: Broadcast on local subnet
Locally stored (primed) WLC IP from previous join
DHCP Option 43 (vendor-specific WLC IPs)
DNS lookup of CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.<localdomain>
Manual / static configuration

front.text: Describe SSO, NSF, and GR and how they work together in router/switch HA.

rows: Synchronizes state between active and standby supervisors so failover is fast and stateful
Continues forwarding using existing FIB during a control plane switchover
Allows routing protocol neighbors to keep forwarding while the device's control plane recovers

front.text: Compare StackWise, VSS, and StackWise Virtual.

rows: Multiple physical switches act as one logical switch via stack cables
Two chassis appear as one logical switch via VSL
Modern equivalent of VSS for distribution/core via SVL

front.text: What is a spine-leaf (Clos) topology and what are its key properties?

items: Two-tier fabric: every leaf connects to every spine; spines do not connect to each other; leaves do not connect to each other
Predictable latency â any host is always 2 hops from any other host
Linear horizontal scale â add spines for bandwidth, add leaves for ports
Used in modern data center fabrics (VXLAN/EVPN) and SD-Access

front.text: When would you choose a 2-tier (collapsed core) over a 3-tier campus design?

back.text: Use a 2-tier (collapsed core) design in small to medium campuses where the distribution and core functions can be handled by the same devices, reducing cost and complexity. Use a 3-tier design (separate access, distribution, and core) in larger campuses with multiple distribution blocks where a dedicated core provides high-speed transit and fault isolation between blocks.

front.text: What is OMP in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN and what does it advertise?

back.text: Overlay Management Protocol (OMP) runs between vSmart controllers and SD-WAN edge routers (and between vSmarts) over DTLS/TLS. It is the SD-WAN control plane and advertises three types of routes: OMP routes (prefixes/VPNs), TLOC routes (transport locators identifying WAN attachments), and service routes (firewalls, IPS chains).

front.text: What is a TLOC in Cisco SD-WAN and what fields uniquely identify one?

back.text: A Transport Locator (TLOC) represents a WAN attachment point on an SD-WAN edge. It is uniquely identified by the tuple: System IP, Color, and Encapsulation (IPsec or GRE). TLOCs are advertised via OMP and used to build IPsec tunnels in the data plane between edges.
Colors (e.g., mpls, biz-internet, public-internet) classify the transport and influence which TLOCs build tunnels with which (private vs public).

front.text: List key benefits of the Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN solution.

items: Transport-independent overlay over MPLS, internet, LTE/5G, etc.
Centralized policy and management via vManage
Application-aware routing using SLA classes
Strong segmentation via VPNs
Built-in zero-trust onboarding (vBond) and end-to-end IPsec
Integrated security (cloud and on-box)

front.text: How does an SD-Access fabric interoperate with a traditional (non-fabric) campus network?

back.text: Interoperation is achieved at the fabric border node, which performs a Layer 3 handoff (and optionally Layer 2 handoff) to traditional networks. The border peers with the external network via standard routing (BGP/OSPF/static), strips VXLAN/LISP encapsulation, and translates SD-Access constructs (VNs, SGTs) into traditional VRFs and possibly VLANs/ACLs in the external domain.

front.text: Describe the three types of SD-Access fabric border nodes.

rows: Known internal areas (data center, WLC, firewall)
Unknown external networks (Internet, public cloud); injects default route
Both known and unknown areas combined on one node

front.text: What is the difference between classification and marking in QoS?

back.text: Classification identifies packets that belong to a particular traffic class based on descriptors (DSCP, ACL, NBAR, ports, etc.) without changing them. Marking modifies a field in the packet (DSCP, IPP, CoS, MPLS EXP) so downstream devices can identify it without re-classifying. Best practice is to classify and mark as close to the source as possible (trust boundary).

front.text: What is the QoS trust boundary and why does it matter?

back.text: The trust boundary is the point in the network where QoS markings (CoS/DSCP) from upstream devices are accepted as valid. Inside the boundary the network honors existing markings; outside, markings are typically reset or remarked. Pushing the trust boundary to the access edge (e.g., trusting a Cisco IP phone via CDP) keeps untrusted user PCs from setting their own DSCP values.

front.text: Compare single-rate two-color, single-rate three-color (srTCM), and two-rate three-color (trTCM) policers.

rows: Differentiates between CIR and a peak rate (PIR)

front.text: What problem does WRED solve compared to default tail drop?

back.text: Tail drop happens when a queue is full and all incoming packets are discarded, which can cause TCP global synchronization (multiple flows back off and ramp up together, oscillating). Weighted Random Early Detection (WRED) probabilistically drops packets before the queue is full, weighted by IP precedence/DSCP, so TCP flows back off at different times and overall throughput remains smooth.

front.text: How does SLAAC allow an IPv6 host to autoconfigure an address?

items: Host generates a link-local address (FE80::/10) and performs DAD
Host sends a Router Solicitation to FF02::2 (all routers)
Router replies with a Router Advertisement (RA) carrying the prefix and L/A flags
Host combines the prefix with an interface ID (EUI-64 or random) to form a global address
Host installs default route via the router that sent the RA

back.text: Stateful info (DNS, etc.) may still come from DHCPv6 if the M or O flag is set in the RA.

front.text: Which FHRP versions support IPv6 and what virtual MAC ranges do they use?
Compare SNMP traps with SNMP informs.
How do you scope a debug to a specific interface or peer using conditional debug?

back.text:
debug condition interface GigabitEthernet0/1
debug ip ospf adj
!
! Or condition by neighbor IP:
debug condition ip 10.1.1.1
debug ip packet
!
! Verify and clear:
show debug condition
undebug all
Conditional debug greatly reduces CPU impact on production routers by limiting debug output to matching events.

front.text: What are the four primary workflow areas in Cisco Catalyst Center?

items: Design â network hierarchy, settings, IP pools, device profiles
Policy â segmentation (VNs), group-based access control with SGTs
Provision â device onboarding, fabric provisioning, templates
Assurance â AI-driven health, issue detection, root cause, ML insights

front.text: A REST API call returns 200 OK with JSON. Which parts of the response do you typically inspect, and how do you authenticate the next call?

items: Status code (200/201 success; 4xx client; 5xx server)
Headers (Content-Type, pagination links, rate-limit info)
Body (JSON/XML payload with the result data)
Token/cookie returned at login is sent on subsequent calls (e.g., X-Auth-Token for Catalyst Center, Authorization: Bearer for OAuth)

front.text: What is MACsec and where does it operate compared to IPsec?

back.text: MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE) provides hop-by-hop Layer 2 encryption and integrity protection between directly connected devices (host-to-switch or switch-to-switch). Unlike IPsec, which encrypts at Layer 3 across multiple hops, MACsec operates per link, so each device decrypts, processes, and re-encrypts the frame. Keys are typically distributed via MKA (MACsec Key Agreement) and can be tied to 802.1X.
MACsec is often paired with TrustSec/802.1X to provide encrypted, authenticated access at the wire.

front.text: How and where do you configure OSPF inter-area and external route summarization?

back.text:
router ospf 1
 area 1 range 10.1.0.0 255.255.0.0
 summary-address 172.16.0.0 255.255.0.0

front.text: What are the main ways to filter routes in OSPF and where do they apply?

rows: Filters Type 3 LSAs in/out of an area
Filters routes installed in RIB (does not stop LSA flooding)

back.text: OSPF is link-state â filtering with distribute-list does not remove LSAs from the LSDB, only blocks RIB installation.

front.text: What does EIGRP stub do and why is it useful?

back.text: EIGRP stub configuration tells the hub that the spoke is a 'stub' router, so the hub will not send EIGRP queries to that spoke. This bounds the query domain and prevents Stuck-In-Active conditions in hub-and-spoke topologies.

router eigrp 100
 eigrp stub connected summary

front.text: What advantages does named-mode EIGRP offer over classic mode?

items: Single hierarchical configuration covering IPv4, IPv6, and VRFs
Per-AF (address-family) and per-interface settings in one place
Easier to read and maintain (af-interface, topology base)
Required for newer EIGRP features (e.g., wide metrics, FRR)

back.text:
router eigrp ENT
 address-family ipv4 unicast autonomous-system 100
  af-interface Gi0/1
   passive-interface
  exit-af-interface
  topology base

front.text: iBGP routes show as valid but not best with 'inaccessible' next-hop. What is the typical cause and fix?

back.text: When an eBGP-learned route is reflected to iBGP peers, the next-hop remains the eBGP peer's address by default. If iBGP peers do not have a route to that next-hop, BGP marks the route as inaccessible. The standard fix is to advertise the link into the IGP, or configure 'neighbor X.X.X.X next-hop-self' on the border router so iBGP peers see the border's loopback as the next-hop.

router bgp 65001
 neighbor 10.0.0.2 remote-as 65001
 neighbor 10.0.0.2 next-hop-self

items: Idle
Connect

OpenSent

front.text: Why are route reflectors used in iBGP, and what loop-prevention attributes do they add?

back.text: iBGP requires a full mesh because routes learned from an iBGP peer are not re-advertised to other iBGP peers. Route reflectors break this rule and reflect routes between clients, eliminating the need for a full mesh and improving scalability. To prevent loops, route reflectors add ORIGINATOR_ID (router-id of originator) and CLUSTER_LIST (RR cluster IDs); if a router sees its own ID in either, it discards the route.

front.text: Area 2 has lost direct connectivity to Area 0 through any ABR. How can you maintain inter-area connectivity, and what are the limitations?

! On both transit ABRs (transit area = 1)
router ospf 1
 area 1 virtual-link 1.1.1.1
Virtual links are a workaround, not a design tool. Stub/totally-stubby/NSSA areas cannot be transit areas for a virtual link.

Switches must agree on the MST region (name, revision, VLAN-to-instance mapping) to share instances; otherwise they appear as separate regions.

front.text: What does UDLD detect and how does it differ from Loop Guard?

back.text: UDLD (UniDirectional Link Detection) is an L2 protocol that detects unidirectional fiber links by exchanging hello frames; if a neighbor stops hearing back, UDLD can put the port in err-disable (aggressive mode). Loop Guard works at the STP level by blocking ports that stop receiving BPDUs. They are complementary: UDLD detects physical unidirectional issues; Loop Guard prevents STP loops from BPDU loss.

front.text: Which 2.4 GHz channels are non-overlapping in North America, and why does this matter?

back.text: In the 2.4 GHz band (channels 1â11 in NA at 22 MHz wide), only channels 1, 6, and 11 are non-overlapping. Using overlapping channels causes co-channel interference and reduces effective throughput because adjacent BSSs cannot transmit simultaneously without collisions.

front.text: What does DHCP snooping protect against, and what is the role of trusted vs untrusted ports?

back.text: DHCP snooping prevents rogue DHCP servers and DHCP starvation attacks by inspecting DHCP messages on a switch. Untrusted ports (typically access ports) drop server-originated messages (OFFER, ACK) and rate-limit DHCP. Trusted ports (uplinks toward legitimate DHCP servers) allow them. The switch builds a binding table (MAC, IP, VLAN, port) used by Dynamic ARP Inspection and IP Source Guard.

front.text: Why is LISP described as a 'pull' routing architecture and what is the main benefit?

back.text: LISP does not flood EID-to-RLOC mappings; instead, ITRs pull mappings on demand from the Map-Resolver/Map-Server when traffic is sent to an unknown destination. The mapping is then cached locally. This eliminates the need for a global routing table at every site, dramatically reducing FIB size and enabling host mobility while keeping the underlay simple.

items: BFD probes measure latency, jitter, and loss on each tunnel/transport
An SLA class defines acceptable thresholds (e.g., voice: latency < 150 ms, loss < 1%, jitter < 50 ms)
An AAR policy maps applications to the SLA class and preferred colors

front.text: What problem does AQM (e.g., CoDel/FQ-CoDel) target that classic WRED does not address well?

back.text: AQM targets bufferbloat â excessive latency caused by oversized buffers staying full under TCP congestion. Classic WRED reacts to queue depth but does not directly measure how long packets sit in the buffer. CoDel measures the minimum queueing delay and drops packets only when delay persists above a target, dramatically reducing latency without sacrificing throughput. FQ-CoDel adds per-flow fairness.

front.text: How is OSPF interface cost calculated by default and how do you adjust it for high-speed links?

back.text: Default cost = reference-bandwidth / interface-bandwidth, where reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps by default. This means anything 100 Mbps or higher gets cost 1, which is a problem for 1G/10G/40G links.

router ospf 1

!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1

front.text: Two OSPF neighbors are stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state. What is the most common cause?

back.text: Mismatched IP MTU on the link. During DBD exchange, OSPF compares MTU values and refuses to progress past EXSTART/EXCHANGE if they differ. Either match the MTUs on both interfaces, or configure 'ip ospf mtu-ignore' under the interface to skip the check.

interface GigabitEthernet0/1

front.text: How do you enable EIGRP unequal-cost load balancing?

back.text:
router eigrp 100
 variance 2
 maximum-paths 4

Variance N allows feasible successors with metric up to N times the successor's FD to be installed in the routing table. Only paths that pass the feasibility condition (RD < FD) are eligible â variance does not bypass loop prevention.

front.text: How do you make a backup static route only become active when the primary OSPF route is lost?

front.text: List the default administrative distances of common routing sources.
Why are route maps and tags commonly used during mutual redistribution between two routing protocols?

back.text: Mutual redistribution at multiple boundary points can create routing loops and suboptimal paths because routes can be redistributed back into their original protocol with a different (often better) administrative distance. Tagging redistributed routes and using route maps to deny re-redistribution of tagged routes prevents loops and contains the routes.

route-map FROM-EIGRP permit 10
 set tag 100
!
router ospf 1
 redistribute eigrp 100 subnets route-map FROM-EIGRP
!
route-map FROM-OSPF deny 10
 match tag 100
route-map FROM-OSPF permit 20
!
router eigrp 100
 redistribute ospf 1 metric 100000 1 255 1 1500 route-map FROM-OSPF

front.text: Why might one link in an EtherChannel carry far more traffic than the others, and how do you fix it?

back.text: EtherChannel uses a hash of selected fields (e.g., src-mac, dst-mac, src-ip, dst-ip, ports) to pick a member link per flow. If many flows hash to the same value (e.g., one big talker, or only L2 hashing on a router-to-router link), traffic is unevenly distributed. Adjust the hashing input to include more variable fields:

port-channel load-balance src-dst-ip
! or for L4 awareness:
port-channel load-balance src-dst-mixed-ip-port

front.text: What is an IPv6 link-local address used for, and what prefix does it use?

back.text: Link-local addresses use the FE80::/10 prefix and are required on every IPv6-enabled interface. They are used for neighbor discovery (NDP), router advertisements, and routing protocol adjacencies (e.g., OSPFv3, EIGRPv6, BGP next-hop on directly connected eBGP). They are not routable beyond the local link.

The classic use case is Anycast RP within a single domain: multiple RPs share a loopback IP, peer with each other via MSDP, and synchronize active source state for redundancy and load sharing.

class-map match-any VIDEO

!

  random-detect

front.text: You have a 100 Mbps interface but only 50 Mbps of CIR from the SP. How do you ensure your QoS policy operates against the 50 Mbps rate?

back.text:
policy-map CHILD
 class VOICE
  priority percent 30
 class class-default
  fair-queue
!
policy-map PARENT
 class class-default
  shape average 50000000
  service-policy CHILD
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 service-policy output PARENT
The parent shapes the aggregate to 50 Mbps; the child policy then performs LLQ/CBWFQ within the shaped rate (HQoS).

front.text: What is WMM and what four access categories does it define?

back.text: WMM (Wi-Fi Multimedia) is a QoS mechanism for 802.11 that approximates 802.1p classes by adjusting CWmin/CWmax/AIFS for each AC, giving VO/VI better channel access.

front.text: How does GLBP provide load balancing that HSRP/VRRP do not?

back.text: GLBP elects an Active Virtual Gateway (AVG) that hands out different virtual MAC addresses to different ARP requests. Each MAC corresponds to an Active Virtual Forwarder (AVF), so multiple routers actively forward traffic for the same virtual IP simultaneously, balancing across them by host. HSRP/VRRP only have one active forwarder per group.

The IP SLA Responder runs on the far-end Cisco device and timestamps packets in hardware/software to provide accurate one-way measurements that pure ICMP cannot.

front.text: What does it mean for an Ansible task to be idempotent, and why is this important for network automation?

back.text: An idempotent task produces the same end-state regardless of how many times it is executed â running it again on a system already in the desired state results in no change. This is critical for network automation because it allows playbooks to be safely re-run without causing duplicate config, churn, or service disruption.

front.text: Why was the NSSA area type created and how are external routes handled inside it?

back.text: Stub areas do not allow Type 5 LSAs, so an ASBR cannot exist there. NSSA was created to allow an ASBR inside a stub-like area. Externals from the NSSA's ASBR are flooded as Type 7 LSAs within the NSSA, then translated to Type 5 LSAs by the ABR when leaving the NSSA into the rest of OSPF.

clear ip bgp 203.0.113.2 soft in
! If route-refresh capability is not supported, enable soft-reconfiguration:
router bgp 65001

Modern peers support BGP route-refresh capability, so 'clear ... soft in' works without storing the full Adj-RIB-In.

front.text: Advertise a /16 aggregate while suppressing the more-specific /24s.

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 address-family ipv4
  aggregate-address 10.10.0.0 255.255.0.0 summary-only

Without 'summary-only', both the aggregate and all components are advertised. With 'as-set', the AS_PATH includes the contributing ASes to prevent loops when the aggregate is announced back into a contributing AS.

front.text: What is BGP MED, and what is the default behavior when comparing MED across different ASes?

back.text: MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator) is a non-transitive hint to a neighboring AS about which entry point to prefer when there are multiple links between two ASes. Lower MED is better. By default, MED is only compared between paths from the same neighbor AS; use 'bgp always-compare-med' to compare MEDs from different ASes (use carefully, as it can cause path oscillation).

front.text: How does AS_PATH provide loop prevention in eBGP, and what is AS_PATH prepending used for?

back.text: When a BGP speaker receives a route, if its own AS appears in the AS_PATH it discards the route, preventing loops between ASes. AS_PATH prepending artificially lengthens the AS_PATH outbound (e.g., 'set as-path prepend 65001 65001') so that path is less preferred by upstream ASes â a common technique to influence inbound traffic engineering.

front.text: What are the private BGP ASN ranges and how do you strip them on egress?

back.text:
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 203.0.113.2 remove-private-as

front.text: Two routers see each other in 'show ip ospf neighbor' as 'INIT' only. What does that indicate?

back.text: INIT means the local router has received a Hello from the neighbor but the neighbor has not yet acknowledged seeing the local router. This typically points to a one-way Hello: an inbound ACL/firewall blocking multicast 224.0.0.5, mismatched authentication on one side, or a unicast-only neighbor not configured on the other side (NBMA).

front.text: What is the difference between the RIB and the FIB?

rows: CEF, derived from the RIB and ARP/ND

back.text: CEF also maintains the adjacency table for L2 rewrite information.

front.text: Why is CEF preferred over process switching and fast switching for production routers?

back.text: CEF (Cisco Express Forwarding) pre-builds a forwarding table (FIB) and adjacency table from the RIB and ARP/ND, so packets can be forwarded in hardware without per-packet route lookup or process-level intervention. It scales linearly, supports load sharing, and is the only switching path supported by most modern features (NetFlow, MPLS, NAT).

front.text: Compare UplinkFast, BackboneFast, and PortFast (legacy 802.1D enhancements).

rows: Fast failover to backup uplink (~3 sec)
Faster recovery from indirect link failures by skipping max-age

back.text: These features are largely obsolete with RSTP/MSTP, which provide native fast convergence.

front.text: Compare VTP versions 1, 2, and 3 in terms of capabilities.

rows: Adds Token Ring VLAN support, transparent forwarding of unknown TLVs
Extended VLANs (1â4094), private VLAN support, primary/secondary servers, MST mapping propagation, password hidden

front.text: What is a Multichassis EtherChannel (MEC) and why is it useful?

back.text: MEC is an EtherChannel where the member links terminate on two physical chassis that act as a single logical switch (VSS, StackWise Virtual, vPC). From the access switch's perspective there is one EtherChannel; from the upstream pair's perspective the link is shared. This eliminates the need for STP-blocked uplinks while providing redundancy and full bandwidth utilization.

front.text: Compare Nexus vPC with Catalyst StackWise Virtual at a high level.

rows: Independent boxes, less risk of split
Shared control plane; SVL loss triggers split-brain detection (Dual-Active)

Easy rollback to a known-good revision
Foundation for CI/CD pipelines and automated testing

front.text: What do CRC, input errors, late collisions, and output drops on an Ethernet interface usually indicate?

rows: Bad cable, faulty SFP, duplex mismatch, EMI
Frame errors of various kinds (CRC, runts, giants)
Duplex mismatch (full/half) or wire too long

front.text: A 100 Mbps link works but performs poorly with high latency under load. What classic L1/L2 issue should you suspect?

back.text: Duplex mismatch (one side full, one side half). Expect late collisions on the half-duplex side, CRC errors on the full-duplex side, and very poor TCP performance under load. Set both sides to either auto/auto or hard-code the same speed and duplex.

front.text: What is TCAM and how is it used in modern switches?

back.text: Ternary Content-Addressable Memory (TCAM) is specialized hardware that returns matches for arbitrary patterns (with don't-care bits) in a single clock cycle. Switches use TCAM for ACLs, QoS classification, FIB longest-prefix match, and policy lookups. TCAM is finite, which is why ACL design and SDM templates matter on switches like the Catalyst 9000.

front.text: Why are SDM templates used on Catalyst switches and how do you change one?

back.text: SDM templates re-allocate finite hardware resources (TCAM, MAC table, routing table sizes) to match a deployment role: access, core/distribution, NAT-heavy, etc. Changing a template typically requires a reload.

show sdm prefer
sdm prefer routing
! reload required

front.text: What is an OID, and how does it relate to a MIB?

back.text: An Object Identifier (OID) is a globally unique numeric path (e.g., 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10) that identifies a specific manageable variable. A MIB (Management Information Base) is the file/document that maps human-readable names (ifInOctets) to OIDs and defines their data types and meaning. SNMP queries use OIDs as keys.

front.text: Why might Cisco IOS traceroute show different results than a Windows tracert to the same destination?

rows: More likely to traverse firewalls allowing ping

back.text: Both rely on incrementing TTL to elicit ICMP Time Exceeded from each hop.

front.text: When is Bidirectional PIM (Bidir-PIM) useful and what is its main characteristic?

back.text: Bidir-PIM is designed for many-to-many multicast (e.g., financial market data, distributed apps with many sources). It uses only shared trees rooted at the RP and never builds (S,G) state, which dramatically reduces multicast routing state. A Designated Forwarder (DF) is elected per link toward the RP to prevent loops.

front.text: What problem does IGMP snooping solve on a Layer 2 switch?

back.text: Without snooping, multicast frames are flooded to all ports in a VLAN like broadcast. IGMP snooping listens to IGMP joins/leaves and builds a per-VLAN, per-group port list, so multicast traffic is only forwarded to ports with interested receivers and the IGMP querier. This prevents wasted bandwidth and CPU on hosts that aren't subscribed.

front.text: Receivers are sending IGMP joins but no multicast traffic arrives. 'show ip mroute' shows 0/0 packets in. What's a likely cause?

back.text: RPF check failure on an upstream router. The router's unicast best path back to the source/RP must come in via the same interface where the multicast traffic arrives; otherwise it is dropped. Verify with 'show ip rpf <source>'. Common fixes: align unicast and multicast routing, use static mroutes, or enable PIM on additional interfaces.

front.text: What replaces IGMP in IPv6 and what well-known IPv6 multicast addresses should you know?

back.text: MLD (Multicast Listener Discovery) replaces IGMP for IPv6 group membership management.

front.text: What are rogue APs and how does Cisco classify them?

rows: Detected AP not on the wired network â likely external

back.text: Rogue Detector and aWIPS features can identify rogues that are on the corporate LAN (most dangerous).

front.text: What is the difference between a stateless and a stateful firewall?

back.text: A stateless firewall (e.g., a basic ACL) evaluates each packet independently against rules. A stateful firewall tracks connections (state table) so return traffic for an allowed outbound flow is automatically permitted, and unsolicited inbound traffic is denied â even if a static rule would have allowed it without state.

front.text: Compare IDS and IPS deployments.

rows: Can drop legitimate traffic if mis-tuned

front.text: What is Cisco Firepower Threat Defense (FTD) and what is FMC?

back.text: FTD is the unified Cisco NGFW image (combining ASA + Firepower services) running on Cisco Secure Firewall appliances. FMC (Firepower / Secure Firewall Management Center) is the centralized off-box manager that provides policy management, IPS rule management, and reporting across many FTD devices. FDM is the on-box manager for smaller deployments.

front.text: How does Cisco Umbrella protect users at the DNS layer?

back.text: Umbrella acts as a recursive DNS resolver in the cloud. When a user queries a domain, Umbrella checks it against threat intelligence and category policies before returning a response. Malicious domains are blocked or redirected to a block page before any TCP connection occurs, stopping malware C2, phishing, and policy-violating sites at the earliest possible step.

front.text: What is the role of Cisco Secure Network Analytics (Stealthwatch) and what data does it consume?

back.text: Secure Network Analytics performs network behavior analysis using flow telemetry (NetFlow, IPFIX, sFlow) and proxy logs. Flow Collectors aggregate data, and the Manager analyzes patterns to detect insider threats, anomalous behavior, and policy violations. Encrypted Traffic Analytics (ETA) can flag malicious encrypted flows without decryption by analyzing TLS handshake characteristics and packet timing.

front.text: What are authentication and authorization policies in Cisco ISE?

rows: Which identity store to use and which protocols are allowed for a request (e.g., 802.1X EAP-TLS vs MAB)
What access result to return after successful auth: VLAN, ACL, SGT, dACL, etc.

front.text: Compare MACsec key derivation modes: pre-shared key (PSK) vs MKA via 802.1X.

rows: Both peers configured with a Connectivity Association Key (CAK); simple, used switch-to-switch
CAK is dynamically derived from EAP authentication via ISE; commonly used host-to-switch

back.text: MKA (MACsec Key Agreement, IEEE 802.1X-2010) is responsible for key distribution and rekeying.

front.text: What is an infrastructure ACL (iACL) and where is it applied?

back.text: An iACL is a hardened ACL applied inbound on edge interfaces (Internet-facing) that protects the router/network infrastructure address space from external attack. It permits only legitimate transit traffic and explicitly denies traffic destined to internal infrastructure subnets, while still allowing return traffic for sessions you initiate. CoPP complements iACLs to protect the local control plane.

front.text: How is multicast typically delivered across an SD-Access fabric?

back.text: Two options: head-end replication (the source's edge node replicates a unicast VXLAN packet to every interested edge), or native multicast in the underlay (PIM-SSM with multicast enabled in the underlay, where receivers' edges join a group). Native multicast scales better; head-end is simpler but consumes more bandwidth at the source.

front.text: What does the wildcard mask in 'network 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 area 0' actually match?

back.text: OSPF compares the network statement against each interface IP. Bits set to 0 in the wildcard must match exactly; bits set to 1 are 'don't care'. Any interface whose IP falls within 10.1.1.0/24 is enabled in area 0. 'network 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 area 0' enables OSPF on all interfaces.

front.text: Show the modern interface-based way to enable OSPF instead of using a network statement.

back.text:
router ospf 1
 router-id 1.1.1.1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf 1 area 0
!
interface Loopback0
 ip ospf 1 area 0
Interface-level activation is preferred â it's explicit and avoids surprises from broad wildcard masks.

front.text: An EIGRP neighbor never forms and the log shows 'K-value mismatch'. What does this mean and how do you fix it?

back.text: Each EIGRP router includes its K1â K5 weights in Hello packets. If they differ between two neighbors, adjacency will not form â even if the metric calculations themselves would be irrelevant. Set 'metric weights 0 K1 K2 K3 K4 K5' identically on both peers (or simply leave defaults: 1 0 1 0 0).

rows: Session shut, ACL block, route to peer missing

Mismatched AS or BGP version negotiation issue
Hold timer expiration, MTU/PMTUD, duplicated update issues

router bgp 65001

This sends 0.0.0.0/0 to the peer regardless of whether you have a default route locally. Use 'default-originate route-map X' to make the advertisement conditional on a local prefix being present.

front.text: Configure MD5 authentication on a BGP neighbor and explain the requirement.

back.text:
router bgp 65001

Both peers must be configured with the exact same password. The password is added to the TCP MD5 signature option (RFC 2385) and authenticates the TCP/179 session, not the BGP messages themselves.

front.text: In a dual-distribution design, why split active FHRP responsibilities per VLAN across the two distribution switches?

back.text: Making each distribution switch the active gateway for half the VLANs balances upstream load across both switches and links. STP root and FHRP active should be aligned (same switch active for the same VLAN) so that traffic does not cross the inter-switch link unnecessarily.

front.text: Compare the roles of LISP and VXLAN in Cisco SD-Access.

rows: Multi-tenant L2/L3 over an IP underlay

front.text: What is VXLAN Group Policy Option (GPO) and why is it used in SD-Access?

back.text: VXLAN-GPO extends standard VXLAN with a 16-bit Group Policy ID field used to carry the Security Group Tag (SGT) end-to-end across the fabric. This allows micro-segmentation policy (SGACLs) to be enforced at the egress edge regardless of the source's IP, since the SGT travels with the encapsulated traffic.

front.text: What is an anycast gateway in an SD-Access fabric and why is it useful?

back.text: Every fabric edge node owns the same SVI IP and MAC for a given subnet. Endpoints always have a gateway that is one hop away regardless of where they connect, and they keep the same default gateway as they roam. This eliminates VLAN-spanning Layer 2 designs and makes mobility seamless.

front.text: How does SD-Access wireless differ from a traditional centralized WLC deployment?

back.text: In SD-Access wireless, only the control plane (CAPWAP) goes to the fabric WLC. Data is forwarded via VXLAN at the AP's fabric edge, just like wired clients â keeping wireless and wired traffic in the same overlay segments and policy. This avoids hairpinning all wireless data through the WLC and uses the fabric's segmentation natively.

front.text: What does Cisco SD-WAN Cloud onRamp provide for SaaS, IaaS, and Multicloud?

rows: Probes multiple paths to a SaaS app (e.g., O365) and steers traffic to the lowest-loss/jitter path
Automates extension of the SD-WAN fabric into AWS/Azure/GCP via cloud gateways
Hosts virtualized SD-WAN/security stack in a colocation facility for branch traffic

front.text: Compare centralized control policy, centralized data policy, and localized policy in Cisco SD-WAN.

rows: Filtering and modifying OMP routes/TLOCs (preferred path, traffic engineering)
Per-flow policy: AAR, service insertion, NAT
Per-device features: ACLs, QoS, route policies on local interfaces

policy-map INGRESS-POL

  police 10000000 187500 conform-action transmit exceed-action drop
!

front.text: List the common classification descriptors used in Cisco QoS.

items: Layer 2: MAC, CoS (802.1p), MPLS EXP

Internal: QoS group (between policies)

front.text: What is IPsec NAT Traversal (NAT-T) and what UDP port does it use?

back.text: ESP (IP protocol 50) cannot survive NAT because PAT requires Layer 4 ports. NAT-T detects NAT in the path during IKE and encapsulates ESP packets in UDP/4500, allowing them to traverse PAT/NAT devices. IKE itself moves from UDP/500 to UDP/4500 once NAT is detected.

front.text: What is Cisco FlexVPN and what are its key advantages over traditional DMVPN?

items: Unified VPN framework using only IKEv2
Supports site-to-site, hub-and-spoke, spoke-to-spoke, and remote access from a single config model
Uses virtual access / virtual template interfaces with per-peer attributes
Native support for AAA-derived per-peer policy
Integrated routing protocols and dynamic spoke-to-spoke tunnels

cards.found_when: GET VPN is discussed in CCNP enterprise security/VPN material

front.text: How does Cisco GET VPN differ from a traditional IPsec tunnel-based VPN?

back.text: GET VPN (Group Encrypted Transport VPN) is tunnel-less. A Key Server distributes a shared group key and policy to all group members. Members preserve original IP headers (no overlay tunnels) and encrypt traffic transparently, which preserves multicast and any-to-any connectivity natively. It is typically used over private WAN/MPLS, not over the public Internet.

front.text: Show a typical IPsec-protected GRE tunnel using a tunnel IPsec profile.

back.text:
crypto ikev2 keyring KR1
 peer R2
  address 198.51.100.2
  pre-shared-key Cisco123
!
crypto ikev2 profile IK2-PROF
 match identity remote address 198.51.100.2 255.255.255.255
 authentication local pre-share
 authentication remote pre-share
 keyring local KR1
!
crypto ipsec profile IPSEC-PROF
 set ikev2-profile IK2-PROF
!
interface Tunnel0
 ip address 172.16.0.1 255.255.255.252
 tunnel source GigabitEthernet0/0
 tunnel destination 198.51.100.2
 tunnel protection ipsec profile IPSEC-PROF

front.text: Why would you use 'ping' in extended mode (ping with no arguments) instead of plain ping?

items: Specify the source interface (test reachability from a particular IP)
Set DF bit and packet size (find PMTU)
Increase repeat count for statistical sampling
Set ToS / DSCP for QoS testing
Use sweep range of sizes for MTU discovery

front.text: How does 'configure replace' differ from 'copy ... running-config'?

back.text: 'copy file running-config' performs a merge â the file's lines are added to the running config but lines not in the file are NOT removed. 'configure replace' performs a rollback â the running config is changed to exactly match the target file, including removing extra lines, by computing and applying a diff. This is what enables true rollback.

front.text: Configure a router for PIM-SM with a static RP.

back.text:
ip multicast-routing
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip pim sparse-mode
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip pim sparse-mode
!
ip pim rp-address 10.10.10.1
Use the same RP address (e.g., a loopback) on every multicast router for consistency.

front.text: Why is 'ip pim autorp listener' commonly required when running Auto-RP with PIM-SM?

back.text: Auto-RP messages are themselves multicast (224.0.1.39 announce, 224.0.1.40 discovery). In pure sparse mode, you need to know the RP first to forward those messages â a chicken-and-egg problem. The autorp listener allows these specific groups to be flooded in dense mode while everything else uses sparse mode.

front.text: What is 'ip multicast helper-map' used for?

back.text: It bridges legacy broadcast applications across a routed network by converting incoming UDP broadcast traffic to multicast (and back to broadcast at the far side), so that broadcast-only applications can be reached over a routed core. It is mostly a legacy feature, useful when modernizing apps is not possible.

front.text: Compare unicast, multicast, and anycast in IPv6 (note: there is no broadcast).

rows: One-to-many â delivered to all members of a group
One-to-nearest â same address on multiple nodes; routed to closest
Does not exist in IPv6 (replaced by all-nodes multicast FF02::1)

front.text: TCP sessions over a tunnel work for small packets but stall for large transfers. What is happening and how do you fix it?

back.text: Tunnels (GRE/IPsec) reduce the effective MTU. If 'ICMP Fragmentation Needed' messages are blocked, PMTUD breaks and large DF-set packets are silently dropped, causing stalls. Fixes: allow ICMP type 3 code 4 in firewalls, and configure 'ip tcp adjust-mss 1360' (or similar) on the tunnel interface so TCP negotiates a smaller MSS that fits inside the tunnel.

front.text: What does LLDP-MED add on top of LLDP that is useful for IP phones?

items: Network policy TLV: voice VLAN, CoS/DSCP for the phone
Power over Ethernet management TLVs (request/granted power)
Inventory TLVs (model, serial)
Location TLV (E911 / building, floor, room)

front.text: Why is it common to disable CDP/LLDP on Internet-facing or untrusted edge interfaces?

back.text: CDP/LLDP advertise platform, IOS version, IP address, and capabilities, which is reconnaissance an attacker can use. Disable them on untrusted interfaces with 'no cdp enable' and 'no lldp transmit/receive', while leaving them enabled internally where they're useful for operations.

monitor capture CAP start
! ... reproduce issue ...

show monitor capture CAP buffer brief

front.text: What is IP Device Tracking (IPDT) and why is it used by features like 802.1X, DAI, and IPSG?

back.text: IPDT (now SISF â Switch Integrated Security Features in newer IOS-XE) builds a binding table of MAC, IP, VLAN, and port for endpoints by snooping ARP/ND/DHCP. This binding table is the source of truth used by Dynamic ARP Inspection, IP Source Guard, IPv6 First-Hop Security, and 802.1X session info.

front.text: What is Cisco Secure Boot / Trust Anchor and why is it important?

back.text: Cisco Secure Boot uses a hardware Trust Anchor module to validate the integrity of every stage of the boot process â microloader, ROMMON, IOS image â against a Cisco-signed digital signature. If any stage is tampered with, the device refuses to boot. This protects against persistent firmware-level compromise even if an attacker has physical access.

When troubleshooting application performance, why does it matter whether the app uses TCP or UDP?

rows: Latency adds jitter but not throughput cap

front.text: Why is an mDNS / Bonjour gateway often deployed in enterprise wireless networks?

back.text: mDNS/Bonjour is link-local (TTL=1) and only works within the same VLAN by default. In modern campuses with separate user, IoT, and printer/AppleTV VLANs, services would not be discovered. The mDNS gateway (on a WLC or switch) listens for service advertisements, caches them, and reissues them across VLAN boundaries based on policy, enabling controlled cross-VLAN service discovery.

front.text: Compare dual-stack, NAT64, and tunneling as IPv6 transition mechanisms.

rows: Run IPv4 and IPv6 in parallel on the same devices
Translates IPv6-only clients to IPv4-only servers
Bridging IPv6 islands across IPv4 cores

front.text: What design principles help an enterprise network scale without redesigning from scratch?

items: Modular hierarchy (access/distribution/core)
Summarize routes at distribution boundaries
Standardize device roles and config templates
Use overlays (VXLAN/SD-Access) to decouple services from physical topology
Plan IP addressing and VLAN ranges with growth in mind
Automate and version-control config (IaC)

front.text: List common resiliency techniques applied at each layer of an enterprise design.

rows: Dual power, dual supervisors (SSO/NSF), redundant fans
EtherChannel, MEC, RSTP/MST, UDLD, Loop Guard
ECMP, fast IGP timers, BFD, FHRP (HSRP/VRRP), graceful restart
Dual circuits, SD-WAN AAR, BGP multihoming

front.text: What is BFD and why is it more efficient than tuning IGP hello/dead timers down to sub-second?

back.text: Bidirectional Forwarding Detection is a lightweight, link-level keepalive protocol that detects forwarding-plane failures in milliseconds. Multiple routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP, HSRP, static routes) can register with BFD and react to a single failure event, rather than each running aggressive hellos. This gives sub-second convergence with much less control-plane CPU load.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 bfd interval 50 min_rx 50 multiplier 3
!
router ospf 1
 bfd all-interfaces

front.text: What is ECMP and how does CEF distribute traffic across multiple equal-cost paths?

back.text: Equal-Cost Multi-Path installs multiple equal-cost next-hops in the FIB. CEF picks an outgoing next-hop using a hash, typically of source/destination IP (and optionally L4 ports), so all packets of a given flow take the same path (avoiding reordering) while different flows distribute across the available links.

front.text: A small campus has 3 buildings, each with one access switch, all aggregating into a single switch pair. Which design model fits best?

back.text: A 2-tier (collapsed-core) design fits well. The pair of switches performs both distribution and core functions, providing L3 boundaries, FHRP, and uplinks. A separate core layer would add cost and complexity without meaningful benefit at this scale.

front.text: A new switch fails Plug-and-Play onboarding to Catalyst Center. What are the most common causes?

items: DHCP not handing out the right Option 43 string for the PnP server
DNS does not resolve pnpserver.<domain> if relying on DNS discovery
No reachability to Catalyst Center (firewall/ACL blocking HTTPS)
Time mismatch causing certificate validation failure
Device serial/PID not pre-claimed in the PnP workflow

front.text: Define the following EIGRP terms: Successor, Feasible Distance (FD), Reported Distance (RD), Feasibility Condition, and Feasible Successor.

rows: The route with the lowest path metric to reach a destination (the best/active route installed in the routing table).
The metric value for the lowest-metric path to reach a destination, calculated locally by the router.
The distance reported by a neighbor to reach a prefix. The RD value is the FD of the advertising router.
A loop-prevention rule: a route is loop-free if the neighbor's RD is less than the local FD.
A backup route that satisfies the feasibility condition (RD < FD), guaranteeing it is loop free.

back.text: Example: If R1's FD to 10.4.4.0/24 via R3→R4 is 3328, and R4 advertises the prefix to R1 with an RD of 2816, the R1→R4 path is a feasible successor because 2816 < 3328.

front.text: List the five EIGRP packet types by opcode value and describe the function of each.

rows: Used to transmit routing and reachability information with other EIGRP neighbors.
Used to request specific routing information from a neighbor.
Sent during the DUAL process when a route is lost and no feasible successor exists, asking neighbors for alternate paths.
Sent in response to a Query, indicating whether the neighbor has an alternate path.
Used for discovery of EIGRP neighbors and for detecting when a neighbor is no longer available.

front.text: Name the five OSPF packet types (by Type number) and describe the purpose of each.

rows: Discover and maintain neighbors; sent periodically on OSPF interfaces.
Summarize LSDB contents during initial adjacency formation.
Request specific LSAs the local router believes are stale or missing.
Carry one or more LSAs as a database update, often in response to an LSR.
Acknowledge received LSAs to provide reliable flooding.

front.text: Compare the OSPF network types: Broadcast, Non-Broadcast, Point-to-Point, Point-to-Multipoint, and Loopback. Include DR/BDR usage and timers.

rows: Frame Relay main interface or multipoint subinterfaces
Not enabled by default; advertises /32 host route, used for hub-and-spoke

front.text: What are the IANA-assigned multicast address ranges for the Local Network Control block, Internetwork Control block, SSM block, and Administratively Scoped block?
What are the IGMPv2 message types for type values 0x16 and 0x11 (general query)?

items: 0x16 — Version 2 Membership Report (commonly called an IGMP join). Used by receivers to join a multicast group or respond to a router's membership query.
0x11 — General Membership Query. Sent periodically to 224.0.0.1 to discover receivers in the attached subnet; group address field set to 0.0.0.0.

front.text: Besides the Type field, what are the other three fields in an IGMP message and what does each carry?

items: Max Response Time — Set only in general/group-specific query messages (0x11). Specifies the maximum allowed time before a responding report, in units of 1/10 second. Set to 0x00 in other messages.
Checksum — 16-bit 1s complement of the 1s complement sum of the IGMP message; standard TCP/IP checksum.
Group Address — 0.0.0.0 in general queries; set to the group address in group-specific queries, membership reports, and leave messages.

front.text: Define the following PIM terms: RPF interface, RPF neighbor, Upstream, and Downstream.

items: RPF interface — The interface used to reach the source of the multicast tree, determined by performing a unicast routing lookup against the source (or RP) IP. Multicast traffic is only accepted on this interface.
RPF neighbor — The next-hop router on the RPF interface (toward the source or RP); PIM joins are sent to this neighbor.
Upstream — Direction toward the source of the tree (actual source for source trees, or RP for shared trees). PIM joins travel upstream.
Downstream — Direction away from the source and toward the receivers.

front.text: What are a First-Hop Router (FHR) and a Last-Hop Router (LHR) in a PIM multicast network?

items: First-Hop Router (FHR) — The router directly connected to the multicast source. It encapsulates source traffic in PIM Register messages to the RP in PIM-SM.
Last-Hop Router (LHR) — The router directly connected to the receivers. It is responsible for sending PIM joins toward the RP or source on behalf of receivers.

front.text: What is the difference between the MRIB, the MFIB, and multicast state?

items: MRIB (Multicast Routing Information Base) — Topology/control-plane table (the mroute table) derived from the unicast RIB and PIM. Contains (S, G), IIF, OIF list, RPF neighbor, and related info.
MFIB (Multicast Forwarding Information Base) — Forwarding/data-plane table programmed from MRIB, used for fast hardware multicast forwarding.
Multicast state — The actual forwarding state used to forward multicast traffic, made up of mroute entries (S, G, IIF, OIF, etc.).

front.text: What traffic descriptors are used at Layer 2, Layer 3, Layer 4, and Layer 7 for QoS classification?

items: Layer 2 — MAC address and 802.1Q/p Class of Service (CoS) bits.
Layer 3 — IP Precedence (IPP), DSCP, and source/destination IP address.
Layer 4 — TCP or UDP ports.
Layer 7 — Application signatures (e.g., NBAR/NBAR2).

front.text: Which marking fields are used at Layer 2 and Layer 3 for QoS?

items: Layer 2 — 802.1Q/p Class of Service (CoS) bits (3 bits in the TCI field).
Layer 3 — Differentiated Services Code Points (DSCP) and IP Precedence (IPP) in the IP header ToS byte.

front.text: The 802.1Q Tag Control Information (TCI) field is 16 bits. What are its three sub-fields and their sizes?

items: Priority Code Point (PCP) — 3 bits; carries the CoS marking.
Drop Eligible Indicator (DEI) — 1 bit; indicates frames that may be dropped first under congestion.
VLAN Identifier (VLAN ID) — 12 bits; identifies the VLAN.

front.text: Define CIR, Tc, Bc, and Token in the context of policing and shaping.

items: CIR (Committed Information Rate) — The rate, in bits per second, that the policer or shaper is configured to enforce as the long-term average.
Tc (Time Committed Interval) — The interval (ms) over which the committed burst (Bc) is sent. Tc = (Bc / CIR) × 1000.
Bc (Committed Burst) — Maximum size of the CIR token bucket in bytes; the maximum amount of traffic allowed in one Tc. Bc = CIR × (Tc / 1000).
Token — A virtual unit (representing bits) that must be available in the bucket to forward a packet; one byte = 8 bits/tokens.

front.text: What are the three common policing algorithms used by Cisco IOS?

items: Single-rate, two-color policer — Single token bucket with conform/exceed actions.
Single-rate, three-color policer (srTCM, RFC 2697) — Adds a Be (excess burst) bucket; conform/exceed/violate actions.
Two-rate, three-color policer (trTCM, RFC 2698) — Two rates (CIR and PIR) and two buckets; conform/exceed/violate actions.

front.text: Briefly describe FIFO, Round Robin, WRR, CQ, PQ, and WFQ legacy queuing algorithms.

rows: Single queue; first packet in is first packet out. All traffic is one class.
Multiple queues serviced one packet at a time in sequence. No starvation, but no prioritization.
Round robin with weights so each queue gets a configurable share of bandwidth.
Cisco WRR implementation with 16 queues, FIFO inside each. Configurable bandwidth per queue.
Four strict-priority queues (high/medium/normal/low). Lower queues only serviced when higher ones are empty — risk of starvation.
Auto-divides bandwidth among flows weighted by IP Precedence; fair, but no fixed-bandwidth guarantee.

front.text: Compare CBWFQ and LLQ. Which is suitable for real-time voice and why?

items: CBWFQ (Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing) — Up to 256 user-defined classes, each with a guaranteed minimum bandwidth during congestion. Provides bandwidth guarantees but no latency guarantee, so it is not suitable alone for real-time voice/video.

LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) — CBWFQ + a strict-priority queue (PQ). The priority queue is serviced first up to its configured bandwidth, providing both latency and bandwidth guarantees for real-time traffic. A built-in policer prevents the priority queue from starving other classes.
Voice traffic should use LLQ's priority queue because it requires both bounded delay/jitter and guaranteed bandwidth.

front.text: Define DES, 3DES, AES, MD5, SHA, DH, RSA, and PSK in the context of IPsec.

items: DES — 56-bit symmetric encryption; very weak, avoid.
3DES — Runs DES three times with three 56-bit keys; deprecated, use AES.
AES — Symmetric encryption with 128/192/256-bit keys, based on Rijndael; preferred.
MD5 — One-way 128-bit hash for data authentication (HMAC-MD5); deprecated.
SHA — One-way 160-bit hash (SHA-1 HMAC); used for data authentication.
DH (Diffie-Hellman) — Asymmetric key-exchange protocol that establishes shared symmetric keys; avoid groups 1/2/5, use 14+.
RSA — Public-key (digital certificate) cryptosystem used to mutually authenticate peers.
PSK (Pre-Shared Key) — A locally configured key used as a credential for mutual peer authentication.

front.text: Compare IKEv1 and IKEv2 in terms of exchange modes, minimum messages to establish IPsec SAs, and authentication.

rows: Main Mode, Aggressive Mode, Quick Mode
Min messages to establish IPsec SAs
PSK, RSA-SIG, Public key — both peers must use the same method
PSK, RSA-SIG; supports asymmetric authentication (each peer can use a different method)
MitM, eavesdropping, plus DoS protection (cookie challenge)

front.text: What are the two methods to encrypt traffic over a GRE tunnel?

items: GRE over IPsec — A GRE tunnel is built first and then protected by an IPsec crypto map or IPsec profile (typically transport mode).
IPsec over GRE — IPsec protects the inner traffic before it is encapsulated into GRE (less common; used in specific designs).

front.text: What are PITRs, PETRs, and PxTRs in LISP?

items: PITR (Proxy ITR) — Acts like an ITR but for non-LISP sites that send traffic to LISP EID destinations.
PETR (Proxy ETR) — Acts like an ETR but for LISP EIDs sending traffic to destinations at non-LISP sites.
PxTR — A router that performs both PITR and PETR functions.

front.text: Name the four VXLAN control and data planes supported by Cisco devices.

items: VXLAN with multicast underlay (flood-and-learn).
VXLAN with static unicast (flood-and-learn) underlay.
VXLAN with MP-BGP EVPN control plane.
VXLAN with LISP control plane (used by Cisco SD-Access).

front.text: What are the roles of the access, distribution, and core layers in a hierarchical LAN design?

items: Access layer — Provides endpoint connectivity (wired/wireless), PoE, port security, and first-hop services for users and devices.
Distribution layer — Aggregates access-layer switches, performs Layer 3 routing, applies policy (ACLs, QoS), and provides redundancy and fast convergence.
Core layer (also called the backbone) — High-speed, low-latency Layer 3 transport between distribution blocks; configuration is kept simple to maximize forwarding performance and stability.

front.text: Name three core capabilities SD-Access brings to a campus network.

items: Automation — Single point of automation, orchestration, and management via Cisco DNA Center; replaces manual device configurations.
Assurance/Analytics — Telemetry-based proactive monitoring and prediction of network and security risks, including encrypted traffic.
Mobility — Seamless host mobility for both wired and wireless clients.
Identity-based Policy — ISE-driven user/device identity for SGACL-based group policies instead of IP/ACL-based rules.
Segmentation/Virtual Networks — One physical infrastructure supports multiple VRFs (virtual networks) for guest, IoT, corporate, etc.

front.text: What are the three types of SD-Access border nodes?

items: Internal border — Connects only to known areas of the organization (e.g., WLC, firewall, data center).
Default border — Connects only to unknown areas outside the organization; configured with a default route for the Internet/public cloud.
Anywhere/Internal+Default border — Combines both functions; connects to known internal networks and to external/transit networks.

front.text: What are the two deployment models for Cisco Secure Cloud Analytics?

items: Public cloud monitoring — Monitors public cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP) using cloud-native telemetry (e.g., VPC flow logs).
Private network monitoring — Monitors on-premises networks using flow data forwarded to the cloud-based analytics service.

front.text: What are the four protocol/framework components used by 802.1X?

items: EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol, RFC 4187) — Provides an encapsulated transport for authentication parameters.
EAP methods — Specific authentication methods used inside EAP (e.g., EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-FAST).
EAPoL (EAP over LAN) — Layer 2 encapsulation defined by 802.1X to transport EAP messages over IEEE 802 wired/wireless networks.
RADIUS — AAA protocol used between the authenticator and the authentication server to carry EAP.

front.text: Compare inline (native) SGT tagging and SXP for propagating SGTs across the network.

items: Inline (native) tagging — Switch inserts a 16-bit SGT directly into the Layer 2 frame (CMD header). Independent of L3 protocol; preserved hop-by-hop. Requires Cisco devices with TrustSec ASIC support; non-supporting devices drop the tagged frame.

SXP (SGT Exchange Protocol) — TCP-based control-plane protocol that communicates IP-to-SGT mappings between devices that lack inline-tagging hardware. Sender is the speaker, receiver is the listener; SXP can be single-hop or multi-hop. The receiving switch enforces policy locally using its IP-to-SGT mapping database.

front.text: What are the three default Cisco IOS privilege levels and what is each capable of?

rows: Allows only disable, enable, exit, help, and logout.
Default after login (R1>); read-only commands; configure terminal NOT allowed.
Highest level (R1#); full access to all CLI commands, including configuration.

front.text: What are the three independent security functions in the AAA framework?

items: Authentication — Verifies user/device identity prior to granting access to a device or service.
Authorization — Defines the access privileges and restrictions enforced for an authenticated user (what they're allowed to do).
Accounting — Tracks/logs user activity (login times, executed commands, etc.) for audit and security reporting.

front.text: What is the difference between a Type 1 and a Type 2 hypervisor? Give examples.

items: Type 1 (Bare-metal) — Runs directly on the physical hardware with no host OS underneath. Used in production data centers. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Cisco NX-OS Hypervisor.
Type 2 (Hosted) — Runs as an application on top of a general-purpose host OS. Used mostly for desktops/labs. Examples: VMware Workstation/Fusion, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop.

front.text: What are the four main components of Cisco Enterprise NFV (ENFV)?

items: Management and Orchestration (MANO) — Cisco DNA Center provides VNF management and NFV orchestration.
Virtual Network Functions (VNFs) — Software providing virtual networking functions (e.g., virtual router, virtual firewall, virtual WLC).
NFV Infrastructure Software (NFVIS) — Operating system that virtualizes the underlying hardware and hosts the VNFs.
Hardware (x86 platform) — x86-based compute resources (CPU, memory, storage) such as Cisco ENCS appliances on which NFVIS and VNFs run.

front.text: In an IPsec transform set, what is the difference between an AH transform and an ESP transform, and what does esp-aes 256 with esp-sha-hmac provide?

items: AH (Authentication Header) transforms — Provide data integrity and origin authentication only; no confidentiality. Examples: ah-md5-hmac (deprecated), ah-sha-hmac, ah-sha256-hmac.
ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) transforms — Provide confidentiality (encryption) and optionally authentication. Examples: esp-aes, esp-aes 256, esp-3des (deprecated), esp-sha-hmac.

esp-aes 256 + esp-sha-hmac — ESP encryption with 256-bit AES plus integrity/authentication using SHA-1 HMAC. Provides confidentiality, integrity, anti-replay, and origin authentication of the payload.
