crypto ipsec transform-set TS_AES256 esp-aes 256 esp-sha-hmac
 mode tunnel

front.text: You configure OSPF on an Ethernet link between two routers and the adjacency stays at 2-WAY between two non-DR routers in a different lab. Why does this happen, and how do you fix it on a point-to-point Ethernet link?

back.text: On Ethernet, OSPF defaults to the Broadcast network type, which elects a DR/BDR. Non-DR/BDR routers (DROther) form a FULL adjacency only with the DR and BDR — with each other they remain at 2-WAY. That is normal behavior for Broadcast networks.

On a point-to-point Ethernet link with only two routers, you can change the OSPF network type to point-to-point so that no DR/BDR is elected, hello/dead timers become 10/40, and the routers form a single FULL adjacency.

interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf network point-to-point
Both ends of the link must agree on the OSPF network type and timers, or the adjacency will not come up.

front.text: R1 has two paths to 10.4.4.0/24: via R3 with FD = 3328, and via R4 with RD = 3500 (and total path metric 3700). Is the path via R4 a feasible successor? Why or why not?

back.text: No. The EIGRP feasibility condition requires the neighbor's reported distance (RD) to be strictly less than the local feasible distance (FD) of the current successor. Here RD via R4 = 3500, but FD = 3328, so RD is NOT less than FD.

Because the FC fails, the path via R4 is not a feasible successor and is not loop-free from R1's perspective. If the successor via R3 is lost, R1 must go ACTIVE for the route and send Queries to neighbors instead of using R4 immediately.
Rule: Feasible Successor exists only when neighbor's RD < local FD.

front.text: What are the EIGRP DUAL Passive and Active states, and what triggers a transition to Active?

items: Passive (P) — Normal/stable state. The route has a successor and DUAL is not actively recomputing it.
Active (A) — DUAL is actively computing a new path because the successor failed and no feasible successor exists. Router sends Queries to neighbors.
Trigger to Active: loss of the successor with no feasible successor in the topology table.
If queries are not answered, the route can become Stuck-In-Active (SIA), causing neighbor reset.

front.text: List the first eight steps of the BGP best-path selection algorithm in order.

items: Prefer the path with the highest WEIGHT (Cisco-only, local to router).
Prefer the path with the highest LOCAL_PREF.
Prefer the path that was locally originated (network or aggregate, redistributed).
Prefer the path with the shortest AS_PATH.
Prefer the path with the lowest ORIGIN type (IGP < EGP < Incomplete).
Prefer the path with the lowest MED (only when AS_PATH first AS is the same).
Prefer eBGP over iBGP paths.
Prefer the path with the lowest IGP metric to the BGP next hop.

back.text: Mnemonic: 'We Love Oranges As Oranges Mean Pure Refreshment' — Weight, Local Pref, Originated, AS-Path, Origin, MED, eBGP/iBGP, IGP.

front.text: Show a basic eBGP configuration between two directly connected routers (R1 in AS 65001, R2 in AS 65002), and the verification command.

back.text:
! On R1 (AS 65001)
router bgp 65001
 neighbor 10.1.1.2 remote-as 65002
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor 10.1.1.2 activate
  network 192.168.1.0 mask 255.255.255.0

! On R2 (AS 65002)
router bgp 65002
 neighbor 10.1.1.1 remote-as 65001

  neighbor 10.1.1.1 activate

show bgp ipv4 unicast summary
show bgp ipv4 unicast neighbors 10.1.1.2

front.text: What are the six HSRP states a router transitions through, and which two are stable in production?

items: Initial — Starting state, before HSRP is enabled or interface comes up.
Learn — Router doesn't know the virtual IP and is waiting to learn it.
Listen — Knows the virtual IP, listens for hellos, but is neither active nor standby.
Speak — Sends hellos and participates in election.
Standby — Backup router; will take over if Active fails.
Active — Forwards packets sent to the virtual IP/MAC.

back.text: Stable production states are Active (one router) and Standby (one router).

front.text: How does PTP (Precision Time Protocol, IEEE 1588) differ from NTP, and where is PTP used?

items: PTP uses hardware timestamps in the network path to achieve sub-microsecond accuracy, while NTP typically achieves only millisecond accuracy.
PTP uses a master/slave/transparent/boundary clock hierarchy; NTP uses stratum-based hierarchy.
PTP works on Ethernet (Layer 2 multicast 01-1B-19-00-00-00) or UDP (Layer 3).
PTP is used in environments needing tight time sync: financial trading, industrial automation, mobile backhaul, and broadcast/media.

front.text: Compare the EtherChannel protocols PAgP and LACP, and list each protocol's modes.
Define the four RSTP port roles: Root, Designated, Alternate, and Backup.

items: Root port — The single port on a non-root switch with the lowest cost path to the root bridge.
Designated port — The port on each segment that forwards traffic toward and away from the root for that segment.
Alternate port — Provides an alternate (backup) path to the root bridge from another switch; goes Forwarding instantly if the root port fails.
Backup port — A backup for a Designated port on the same switch (only on shared/hub segments).

front.text: Compare BPDU Guard, BPDU Filter, Root Guard, and Loop Guard.

rows: Disables (err-disables) a PortFast-enabled access port if it receives any BPDU. Used on user-facing ports.
Suppresses sending and/or receiving BPDUs on a port. Use carefully — can break STP if misapplied.
If a superior BPDU is received on the port, the port is moved to root-inconsistent (blocking). Used on ports facing other switches that should never become root.
If BPDUs stop being received on a non-designated port, port goes loop-inconsistent (blocking) instead of becoming designated forwarding. Prevents unidirectional link loops.

front.text: What are the DTP modes, and which combinations form a trunk?

rows: Always trunk; sends DTP unless nonegotiate.
Actively tries to form a trunk via DTP.
Will become a trunk only if the other side is Trunk or Desirable.

items: Trunk forms with: Trunk-Trunk, Trunk-Desirable, Trunk-Auto, Desirable-Desirable, Desirable-Auto.
Does NOT form trunk: Auto-Auto, Access-anything.
Best practice: hard-code mode (access or trunk) and use switchport nonegotiate to disable DTP.

front.text: What problem does MST (Multiple Spanning Tree, 802.1s) solve, and what three values must match for switches to be in the same MST region?

items: Problem: PVST+ runs one STP instance per VLAN, which doesn't scale at high VLAN counts. MST maps many VLANs into a few instances to reduce CPU/memory load.
Switches must agree on these three values to be in the same MST region:
1. MST configuration name
2. MST revision number
3. VLAN-to-instance mapping table

back.text:
spanning-tree mode mst
spanning-tree mst configuration
 name CAMPUS
 revision 1
 instance 1 vlan 10,20
 instance 2 vlan 30,40

front.text: List the steps of a LISP packet flow when a host in Site A sends traffic to a host in Site B.

items: Source host sends packet to its default gateway (the ITR), destined for the remote EID.
ITR checks its EID-to-RLOC map cache. If no entry exists, ITR sends a Map-Request to the Map Resolver (MR).
MR forwards the request to the Map Server (MS), which finds the registered ETR for the destination EID.
ETR responds with a Map-Reply containing the EID-to-RLOC mapping back to the ITR.
ITR caches the mapping, then LISP-encapsulates the packet (IP-in-UDP, port 4341) with the ETR's RLOC as the outer destination.
Underlay routes the encapsulated packet to the ETR.
ETR de-encapsulates and forwards the original packet to the destination EID.

front.text: What is a QoS trust boundary, and where should it typically be placed?

items: A trust boundary is the demarcation between where QoS markings (CoS/DSCP) coming from a device can be trusted vs. where they should be rewritten.
Beyond the trust boundary (toward the network), markings are honored without modification.
At/before the trust boundary (toward the user), markings are typically classified, remarked, or zeroed.
Best practice: trust boundary at the access layer, ideally on the access switch port for trusted devices (Cisco IP phones), or as deep into the network as you can place it.

front.text: Compare traffic shaping and traffic policing.

rows: Buffers (delays) traffic to smooth output to CIR
Drops or remarks traffic above CIR
Can cause TCP retransmits and bursts
Sub-rate WAN circuits to match SP-enforced rate
Enforce contract rate at ingress, drop abusive flows

front.text: How many bits are in CoS, IP Precedence, and DSCP, and where in the frame/packet are they located?

rows: 802.1Q TCI field in Ethernet frame (only on tagged trunks)
First 3 bits of the ToS byte in the IPv4 header
First 6 bits of the DS field (replaces ToS) in IPv4 or Traffic Class in IPv6

front.text: Compare the three main lightweight AP operating modes: Local, FlexConnect, and Bridge/Mesh.

items: Local mode — Default. AP tunnels all client traffic via CAPWAP back to the WLC; centralized data plane.
FlexConnect (formerly H-REAP) — AP can locally switch client traffic to the wired LAN even if the WAN to the WLC fails. Used for branch offices.
Bridge / Mesh mode — AP acts as a wireless bridge; used for outdoor mesh networks, with a Root AP connecting back to the wired network and Mesh APs forming wireless backhaul.

front.text: What UDP ports does CAPWAP use, and what does each tunnel carry?

rows: CAPWAP data plane (client traffic to/from WLC)

front.text: What are the four key configuration components of Flexible NetFlow?

items: Flow Record — Defines the key (match) and non-key (collect) fields used to identify a flow.
Flow Exporter — Defines the destination collector IP, transport protocol/port, and source interface.
Flow Monitor — Combines a record + exporter(s) and a cache for flow data.
Apply to interface — flow monitor MON input/output applies the monitor to traffic.

back.text:
flow record REC1
 match ipv4 source address
 match ipv4 destination address
 collect counter bytes
 collect counter packets
!
flow exporter EXP1
 destination 10.1.1.10
 transport udp 9996
!
flow monitor MON1
 record REC1
 exporter EXP1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip flow monitor MON1 input

front.text: How does Cisco Encrypted Traffic Analytics (ETA) detect threats in encrypted traffic without decrypting it?

items: ETA exports enhanced telemetry from Cisco switches/routers (Catalyst, ISR/ASR) for encrypted flows.
Three key data elements collected: Initial Data Packet (IDP) of the flow, Sequence of Packet Lengths and Times (SPLT), and TLS-specific metadata (cipher suites, key exchange).
Cisco Secure Network Analytics + Secure Cloud Analytics use machine learning on these features to identify malware patterns in encrypted traffic.
Result: detect threats and verify TLS compliance without breaking end-to-end encryption.

front.text: What are the four primary workflow areas in Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center)?

items: Design — Build network hierarchies, set global settings (DNS, NTP, AAA), and define IP address pools.
Policy — Define group-based access policies (using ISE/TrustSec) and create virtual networks.
Provision — Push configurations and onboard devices using PnP, including SD-Access fabric provisioning.
Assurance — Telemetry-driven monitoring, AI-powered insights, and proactive issue detection for clients, network, and applications.

front.text: Two OSPF routers are stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state and the adjacency never reaches FULL. What is the most common cause and fix?

items: Most common cause: MTU mismatch between the two interfaces. OSPF requires matching MTU to negotiate database descriptor (DBD) packets.
When MTUs differ, the side with the larger MTU sends DBDs the other side can't accept, and the FSM stalls at EXSTART/EXCHANGE.
Fix 1 (preferred): Match the MTU on both interfaces (e.g., both 1500 or both 9000).
Fix 2: Disable MTU check on the OSPF interface — but this is a workaround, not a real fix:

back.text:
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip ospf mtu-ignore
Verify with: show ip ospf neighbor, show ip ospf interface, and show interfaces (look at MTU).

front.text: An eBGP session between two directly connected routers is stuck in Active state. List the most common causes.

items: TCP port 179 blocked — ACL or firewall preventing the TCP three-way handshake.
Wrong neighbor IP or remote-as configured on either side.
Source IP mismatch — eBGP peer expects a different source. Use 'neighbor X.X.X.X update-source <int>' if peering loopback-to-loopback (and adjust ebgp-multihop).
Default eBGP TTL = 1; if peering between loopbacks (not directly connected), need 'ebgp-multihop' or 'disable-connected-check'.
No IGP route or static route to reach the neighbor IP.
Authentication password mismatch (MD5).
Neighbor not yet activated under the address-family.

back.text:
show bgp ipv4 unicast summary
show ip route 10.1.1.2
debug ip tcp transactions
debug bgp ipv4 unicast events

front.text: You have a hub-and-spoke topology over a multipoint Frame Relay subinterface. Spokes can ping the hub but not each other through OSPF. Which OSPF network type would you choose, and why?

items: Use OSPF point-to-multipoint network type.

Why: P2MP advertises each spoke as a /32 host route with the next hop set to the spoke's own interface. This avoids the partial-mesh next-hop problem you hit with broadcast/non-broadcast on a hub-and-spoke NBMA, where the hub doesn't change the next hop on advertised routes.
P2MP also does not require DR/BDR election (since not all spokes have direct PVCs to each other).
Timers under P2MP: Hello 30 / Dead 120.

back.text:
interface Serial0/0.1 multipoint
 ip ospf network point-to-multipoint

front.text: You want to make Distribution-Switch-1 the root for VLANs 10–20 and Distribution-Switch-2 the root for VLANs 30–40, with each acting as backup for the other. Show the configuration.

back.text:
! On Distribution-Switch-1
spanning-tree vlan 10-20 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 30-40 root secondary
!
! On Distribution-Switch-2
spanning-tree vlan 30-40 root primary
spanning-tree vlan 10-20 root secondary

items: spanning-tree vlan X root primary sets the bridge priority to 24576 (or 4096 lower than current root).
spanning-tree vlan X root secondary sets the bridge priority to 28672.
This pattern is the classic per-VLAN load-balancing design with PVST+/Rapid-PVST+.
Verify with: show spanning-tree vlan 10.

front.text: What is Policy-Based Routing (PBR), and what makes it different from normal destination-based routing?

items: PBR uses a route-map applied to an interface (or locally) to make forwarding decisions based on attributes other than just destination IP — e.g., source IP, source/destination port, packet size, or DSCP.
Normal routing only looks at destination IP via the routing table.
Use cases: send specific traffic out a backup link, route VoIP via a low-latency path, force certain users through a security device.
Applied with: ip policy route-map <NAME> on the ingress interface, or ip local policy route-map <NAME> for router-originated traffic.

back.text:
access-list 100 permit ip 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255 any
route-map PBR_VOICE permit 10
 match ip address 100
 set ip next-hop 192.0.2.1
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 ip policy route-map PBR_VOICE
